headline: Feds sue over NJ order barring ICE from some state property
summary: The Trump administration is suing New Jersey over Governor Mikie Sherrill's executive order that restricts federal immigration agents from entering private areas of state buildings without a judicial warrant and from using state property for immigration enforcement operations. The federal government argues the order violates the supremacy clause and discriminates against ICE and CBP. New Jersey defends the policy as necessary to protect constitutional rights and immigrant communities, citing documented ICE violations of court orders and reduced agent training.

headline: Kentucky would opt into federal plan for private-school tuition aid under GOP bill
summary: Kentucky's House Appropriations and Revenue Committee approved House Bill 1 to enroll the state in a federal program offering K-12 students up to $1,700 annually in tax credit scholarships through scholarship granting organizations, with administration by the Secretary of State. Republicans claim this expands school choice without state costs, while Democrats criticize it as bypassing recent Supreme Court and voter rejections of private school funding. The legislation emerged after the Court struck down a 2022 charter school law, representing a shift to federal mechanisms for education choice. The committee vote split along partisan lines, with 16 Republicans supporting and Democrats opposing or abstaining. The bill reflects ongoing political conflict over education funding and school choice in Kentucky.

headline: Senate plan would spend $254M to improve reading scores, raise Oklahoma teacher pay
summary: Senate Republican leadership proposes a $254 million K-12 education plan funded by capping teacher pension apportionment funds at $200 million, redirecting $254 million from pensions to education while slowing full pension funding by five years without affecting benefits. The plan includes a $2,500 salary increase for all public school teachers, $50 million to triple literacy funding through the Strong Readers Act, $10 million each for literacy and math coaching programs, and $25 million to expand private school tax credits. Democrats oppose the pension cap and private school funding, arguing it risks cost-of-living adjustments for retirees and disproportionately benefits wealthy families. The proposal responds to Oklahoma's low reading proficiency, with only 26% of students scoring at grade level in English language arts. Senate leaders describe the plan as revenue-neutral and essential for addressing what they call a "new emergency" in public education.

headline: Man who says lack of treatment for broken arm caused amputation settles lawsuit against prison health provider
summary: A Mississippi prison healthcare case where a man's broken arm developed sepsis requiring amputation after alleged delayed treatment has prompted legislative discussion of House Bill 1740, which would establish communal kiosks for prisoners to request medical care, highlighting systemic failures in the state's contracted prison health system and raising public health concerns about healthcare access and infection control in correctional facilities.

headline: Eyeball recognition, surveillance towers part of $40 million immigration awards for local police
summary: Gov. Ron DeSantis and the Florida Cabinet approved over $40 million in new immigration enforcement grants for local law enforcement, part of a $250 million state program that has spent $573 million since 2023 to support a sweeping crackdown on illegal immigration. The funding flows through the newly created State Board of Immigration Enforcement, which has approved 101 grant requests totaling $60 million since September but disbursed only $93,544, revealing a major implementation gap. The grants prioritize AI-driven surveillance technology, biometric identification systems, and detention infrastructure, effectively deputizing all county sheriffs as immigration agents. Florida officials are still awaiting more than $600 million in promised federal reimbursement that federal lawyers now dispute was ever guaranteed, creating a potential fiscal liability for the state. The policy framework establishes a state-level immigration enforcement apparatus that operates parallel to federal authorities, with local agencies requesting equipment ranging from license plate readers and rapid DNA machines to ballistic vehicle doors and body scanners for detainees.

headline: Florida GOP voters say a DeSantis endorsement for governor would mean more than Trump’s
summary: A University of North Florida poll of 657 likely Florida Republican voters (Feb. 16-20, +/-4.38% MOE) reveals that informing voters of Donald Trump's endorsement boosts Byron Donalds' support from 28% to 47% in the gubernatorial race, while Casey DeSantis drops from 24% to 12%. The survey demonstrates Trump's endorsement has effectively frozen the Republican primary, mirroring his 2018 impact on Ron DeSantis's campaign. When asked whose endorsement matters more, 42% of respondents chose Ron DeSantis over Trump's 32%, though analysts suggest this reflects curiosity about the unannounced endorsement rather than preference. The poll indicates that Trump's public backing creates a decisive advantage before formal campaigning begins. The results highlight how early endorsements can shape candidate viability and donor calculations in Florida's political landscape.

headline: Proposed budgets defund Florida Forever, but do provide for conservation easements
summary: The Florida House's FY 2026/27 budget eliminates Florida Forever funding while the Senate allocates $35 million for easements only, diverting $300 million and $200 million respectively to the Rural and Family Lands Protection program—a policy shift Republicans justify as reducing taxpayer maintenance burdens. Governor DeSantis has proposed $115 million for Florida Forever, creating a significant budgetary conflict with the Legislature. The state's $115 billion total budget means even the governor's proposal represents less than 0.1% of spending. Environmental groups warn this funding reduction could cause critical conservation properties to be lost to development, despite 78-83% voter support for $100 million in funding. The debate reflects a broader philosophical divide over direct land acquisition versus conservation easements with different fiscal implications for state taxpayers.

headline: Detroit chamber poll: Two-thirds of Michigan voters don’t have a strong position on AI data centers
summary: A Glengariff Group poll of 600 Michigan voters commissioned by the Detroit Regional Chamber found 67% lack strong opinions on AI data centers, with only 5% strongly supporting and 25% strongly opposing them. Awareness varies significantly by demographic: 70.2% of college-educated voters had heard about data centers versus 27.2% of Black voters and 33.3% of those earning under $30,000. Among opponents, 36.8% cite environmental/water concerns and 16.1% worry about electricity shortages. The poll reveals a 2-to-1 margin (36.4% vs 20.3%) believe data centers offer economic benefits, primarily through job creation. These findings shape Michigan's policy debate as lawmakers consider rescinding tax credits or imposing moratoriums, while the Chamber uses the data to advocate for data centers as essential infrastructure.

headline: Hundreds rally as Romulus council rejects ICE detention center plan
summary: Hundreds of protesters gathered outside Romulus City Hall on Feb. 23, 2026, to oppose a planned ICE detention center, prompting the Romulus City Council to unanimously pass a formal resolution against the facility and send it to federal, state, and local representatives. State lawmakers and candidates, including State Sens. Mallory McMorrow and Darrin Camilleri and State Reps. Dylan Wegela and Donavan McKinney, spoke at the rally, calling for ICE abolition and warning the facility would harm community safety and property values. Counterprotesters clashed briefly with demonstrators before police intervened, while protesters chanted anti-ICE slogans for over two hours, with many unable to enter the council meeting due to limited capacity. The event served as political mobilization, with Lt. Gov. Garlin Gilchrist urging attendees to translate protest energy into votes in the November election.

headline: Detroit Police Department officer suspended over immigration call faced prior federal claims
summary: Two Detroit police officers were suspended for 30 days without pay after contacting Border Patrol during traffic stops, violating department policy that prohibits local enforcement of federal immigration laws or using Border Patrol for translation services; one officer is suing to retain her position, highlighting tensions between municipal policy limiting police involvement in immigration enforcement and departmental accountability measures.

headline: Ayotte says DHS is backing away from plans for an immigrant detention center in Merrimack
summary: The Department of Homeland Security canceled plans for an ICE immigrant detention facility in Merrimack, New Hampshire, that would have temporarily detained immigrants before deportation as part of federal mass deportation efforts. The proposal generated significant protests and political opposition from local officials and created a political challenge for Governor Kelly Ayotte, who initially denied knowledge but later faced criticism after documents showed state agency staff had communicated with federal officials. The controversy led to the resignation of a department commissioner and accusations from Democrats that Ayotte was either incompetent or dishonest. Following discussions between Ayotte and DHS Secretary Kristi Noem, the project was scrapped. Both officials emphasized continued cooperation between New Hampshire and DHS on border security and law enforcement.

headline: Part 6:  Seven fiscal headwinds facing Delaware — and how understanding the budget helps us plan ahead
summary: Delaware's FY2026 budget faces seven headwinds: spending grew 7.3% to $6.5 billion, exceeding sustainable 4% targets; the state relies on corporate franchise revenue for 25-30% of its General Fund, creating concentration risk amid slowing 1.9% GDP growth; rising fixed costs in healthcare and pensions strain resources; Gov. Meyer's FY2027 proposal aims for 5% growth restraint while lawmakers debate stalled revenue diversification efforts like income-tax restructuring; strong reserves of $830 million provide cushion but don't eliminate structural imbalance risks.

headline: Nevadans to Trump: Where’s our money?
summary: Nevada state Treasurer Zach Conine estimates unconstitutional federal tariffs have cost Nevada workers and families $2.1 billion, potentially reducing state tax revenues and economic activity that inform budget forecasts. Democratic officials including Attorney General Aaron Ford, Senator Jacky Rosen, and Representative Steven Horsford are pursuing federal refunds to mitigate these economic damages. Governor Joe Lombardo and Nevada Republicans have not challenged the tariff policy, creating political division over the state's fiscal response. The Supreme Court's ruling provides potential relief, but ongoing federal uncertainty continues to cloud Nevada's revenue projections. State budget implications remain unclear as federal tariff policy evolves and congressional action on refunds remains uncertain.

headline: House tees up sprawling energy package that would cut $1B from Mass Save
summary: Massachusetts House Democrats are advancing an energy bill that cuts $1 billion from the Mass Save program's $4.5 billion budget, reflecting state-level fiscal decisions to reduce ratepayer-funded expenditures amid affordability concerns. The legislation, projected to save ratepayers nearly $9 billion over a decade, demonstrates how lawmakers are balancing climate commitments with budgetary constraints by requiring an inspector general review of program efficiency. Governor Healey's proposal to spend $180 million to subsidize utility bills this winter further illustrates state intervention in energy costs. These actions represent significant state policy choices about resource allocation, program financing, and fiscal responsibility in publicly-mandated initiatives, even though Mass Save is funded through utility charges rather than direct state appropriations. The debate highlights the political challenges of managing program budgets while maintaining climate targets during a period of high energy costs.

headline: Milwaukee continues preparing for possible ICE surge
summary: Milwaukee aldermen proposed ordinances requiring ICE agents to be unmasked and prohibiting raids on county property, responding to community fears sparked by Minnesota's Operation Metro Surge. The Milwaukee Police Department and County Sheriff's Office maintain policies of non-cooperation with immigration enforcement, contrasting with neighboring Waukesha and Washington counties that have 287(g) agreements deputizing local law enforcement. Local officials are questioning whether Milwaukee could independently investigate potential misconduct by federal agents, following Minnesota's experience where state investigators were blocked from accessing scenes where ICE agents killed civilians. The proposals face uncertain legal viability but reflect strong community demand for protective measures, as evidenced by packed town halls and a letter campaign generating over 1,800 messages to the Common Council. A bilingual community discussion is scheduled to prepare residents, emphasizing transparency and rights awareness amid concerns about potential ICE surges.

headline: Some Idaho lawmakers called this Medicaid cut an accident. But a bill to undo the cut is stalling.
summary: Idaho's tight budget climate is blocking reinstatement of the Healthy Connections Medicaid program, which was eliminated in last year's cost-cutting legislation; reinstating the program would require $6.3 million in state funds but generate $150-400 million in annual savings, yet Joint Finance-Appropriations Committee members state no funding is available, prompting one senator to suggest tapping rainy day funds as the program serves as a critical cost-containment tool for the state's Medicaid budget.

headline: Tennessee House Republican opposes ICE detention facility for Lebanon
summary: Tennessee Republican officials, including State Rep. Clark Boyd and U.S. Sen. Marsha Blackburn, are opposing a proposed ICE detention center in Lebanon, Wilson County, citing infrastructure limitations, public safety concerns, and incompatibility with the area's low unemployment and economic development goals. While supporting ICE's enforcement mission, they argue the Highway 109 location near schools and churches is unsuitable. Wilson County officials claim ICE is considering a 14,000-16,000 bed "mega center," though State Sen. Mark Pody disputes this as "misinformation" and says no purchase agreement exists. Local law enforcement opposes the strain on emergency services. The conflict illustrates local Republican pushback against federal immigration enforcement placement decisions despite ideological alignment.

headline: State lawmakers seek restraints on wage garnishment for medical debt
summary: Colorado legislation banning medical debt wage garnishment could strain healthcare provider finances, particularly for rural hospitals, potentially requiring increased state budget allocations to sustain healthcare access and cover provider losses, while shifting collection burdens from individuals to the state healthcare system.

headline: Colorado’s DeGette plans to skip Trump’s State of the Union address
summary: Several Democratic members of Congress, including Colorado Rep. Diana DeGette, are boycotting President Trump's State of the Union address as political protest; alternative protest events include an outdoor "People's State of the Union" rally and a "State of the Swamp" gathering in Washington, D.C., with some lawmakers like Rep. Jason Crow attending both the presidential address and the protest events.

headline: Senators propose phasing out data center tax credit, House budget keeps it
summary: The Virginia House and Senate proposed competing amendments to the $212 billion state budget regarding data center sales tax exemptions, creating a major fiscal policy dispute. The Senate plan would phase out the exemption by 2027 to generate $1.6 billion annually for transportation and water infrastructure, while the House version maintains the exemption with new clean energy requirements and the governor proposed extending it through 2050. A legislative audit found the industry provides $2.1 billion in net positive tax revenue over five years, though the state exempted $3.2 billion in equipment purchases in fiscal years 2024-2025. The chambers will vote on their respective budgets before reconciling differences in a closed-door conference committee, with the outcome significantly affecting state revenue forecasts and infrastructure funding priorities.

headline: HMSA Proposal: Hawaiʻi Health Care Could See Radical Transformation
summary: Potential policy changes at Hawaii Medical Service Association (HMSA) could affect 760,000 members and their physicians statewide. This relates to public health by altering healthcare access, insurance coverage, and medical service delivery for a large portion of Hawaii's population.

headline: Climate Funding Under New Hawai‘i Tax Could Be Cut By Millions
summary: Legislation shifting cruise ship fees from a green fee to a per-passenger structure would generate less than half the expected state revenue, significantly reducing budget contributions from the cruise industry and requiring Hawaii to adjust revenue forecasts and potentially identify alternative funding sources.

headline: Maryland Democrats to protest Trump’s State of the Union with their presence, absence
summary: Democratic members of Maryland's congressional delegation are protesting President Trump's State of the Union address through boycotts by four lawmakers and counter-programming events like the People's State of the Union organized by liberal groups around the Capitol, while attendees bring guests affected by administration policies as silent protest. The article references last year's disruptive protests where Democrats held up paddles with messages, walked out, and Rep. Al Green was removed and censured for a shouting outburst. House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries framed the strategy as "attend with silent defiance, or to not attend," with boycotting members like Rep. Glenn Ivey focusing on alternative messaging to constituents about healthcare costs and immigration policy. These organized actions constitute political unrest during a major government address, reflecting Democratic opposition to Trump administration policies during a partial government shutdown and after Supreme Court rulings against his tariffs.

headline: Maryland sues to block ‘unlawful’ immigration detention center in Washington County
summary: Maryland Attorney General Anthony Brown sued to block a Trump administration plan to convert a Washington County warehouse into a 1,500-bed ICE detention center, alleging violations of environmental and administrative procedure laws without required reviews or public consultation. The Department of Homeland Security defended the facility as essential for public safety and criticized the lawsuit as political obstruction. Governor Wes Moore signed emergency legislation terminating 287(g) agreements between ICE and nine Maryland counties, while state lawmakers advanced additional bills to further restrict ICE cooperation, including limits on data sharing and citizenship inquiries.

headline: Data center tax breaks are on the chopping block in some states
summary: States are reevaluating data center tax incentives due to significant revenue losses, with Virginia forfeiting $1.6 billion and Georgia projecting $2.5 billion in losses this year; lawmakers in Maryland, Michigan, Arizona, and Georgia have introduced legislation to repeal or restrict these exemptions, reflecting budget pressures and concerns over rising electricity costs and environmental impacts, while industry groups warn that abrupt policy changes create market uncertainty for long-term capital investments.

headline: Kansas cannot fund a bigger mental health workforce and then let plans shrink the network
summary: Kansas's state budget invested nearly $6 million to expand the mental health workforce, but Medicaid managed care organizations are undermining this by terminating provider contracts "without cause" to balance their own budgets, reducing network capacity in 96 shortage counties. This budget-cutting strategy by MCOs directly contradicts the state's policy goals and legislative efforts to strengthen behavioral health access through workforce training, Certified Community Behavioral Health Clinics, and 988 crisis response funding. The legislature is considering HB 2753 to restrict MCOs from terminating providers they cited for network adequacy during the first 24 months of a contract, except for cause. The conflict between state budget investments in capacity-building and MCO budget practices that shrink provider networks creates a policy contradiction where public funds fail to achieve intended access outcomes. State budget accountability requires transparent reporting of provider terminations and tying MCO performance to behavioral health access outcomes rather than just cost containment.

headline: No rest for the part-time at CSCU
summary: The article describes systemic labor grievances among Connecticut's part-time state college faculty—no paid sick leave, no job security—that historically trigger worker protests and civil unrest. With 58% of CSCU courses taught by these instructors and existing union organization (CSU-AAUP), failure by state policymakers to address these conditions could catalyze future demonstrations or strikes.

headline: Connecticut’s education crisis
summary: Connecticut's K-12 education system faces a critical teacher retention crisis, with 98% of educators reporting stress and burnout, 74% considering early departure, and 11 subject shortage areas; despite state programs like alternate certification and mentoring, teachers earn starting salaries of $49,860—far below the state's $90,897 minimum living wage—while Massachusetts guarantees higher pay and $5,000 bonuses, enabling teachers to exceed living wage thresholds; 70% of Connecticut teachers now discourage others from entering the profession, with Alliance districts experiencing the most severe shortages; policymakers must address systemic issues through base pay increases, reduced administrative burdens, and enhanced mental health support to prevent instructional quality decline and institutional knowledge loss.

headline: Governor-backed plan advances as Legislature continues to cull property tax reduction ideas
summary: The South Dakota Senate narrowly rejected Senate Bill 199, which would have diverted 25% of annual state general fund revenue growth toward property tax relief, signaling legislative caution about committing state budget resources to tax reductions; this failure contrasts with advancing county-level sales tax proposals, indicating policymakers prefer local revenue mechanisms over direct state budget allocations for property tax relief.

headline: State House decides to retain automatic employer fees for governor-controlled economic fund
summary: A South Dakota House bill to make voluntary the employer fee financing the governor-controlled Future Fund failed 31-36, raising state budget concerns. The fee, collected via unemployment payroll taxes, is a revenue source for economic development programs including the Build Dakota Scholarship Fund. Opponents warned the change would reduce funding for these initiatives. The bill's defeat maintains automatic fee collection and preserves current revenue levels. This decision protects existing budget allocations for economic development programs from potential cuts.

headline: Missouri House passes supplemental spending bill that pushes state budget to $55 billion
summary: Missouri's House approved a $3.15 billion supplemental spending bill for FY2025, raising total appropriations to $55 billion, while projected revenue declines will exhaust the state's general revenue surplus from $5.8 billion to $265 million by 2027. For the upcoming FY2026 budget, Republican lawmakers propose $2 billion in cuts, including $500 million in general revenue from health, mental health, and social services departments, triggering an additional $1.5-2 billion loss in federal matching funds. The cuts come despite the House allocating $76.7 million in state funds for Medicaid expansion for the first time since 2020. State Rep. Darin Chappell, chairing the relevant budget subcommittee, acknowledged the cuts are necessary due to fiscal constraints but pledged to restore proposed reductions to disability care and substance use disorder services. The budget tension reflects Missouri's contracting fiscal outlook, with general revenues forecasted to drop 2.1% this year despite currently running 1.7% above prior year levels.

headline: WA governor’s $1B borrowing plan to buy new ferries sinks in Legislature
summary: Washington Legislature's transportation budget proposals rejected Governor Bob Ferguson's $1 billion borrowing request for three additional hybrid electric ferries, citing competing priorities including highway safety, maintenance, bridge preservation, a multibillion-dollar tribal culvert settlement, and flood damage repairs. Both chambers released separate operating budget proposals over the weekend, with budget writers emphasizing the need to balance ferry fleet modernization with other major spending demands. The proposals further paused funding for converting existing diesel ferries to electric power through at least 2031. In response to fiscal constraints, lawmakers are exploring capital-efficient alternatives such as leasing vessels, with the Senate approving a consultant study for hydrogen ferry leasing. The budgets did include modest investments like $2 million for a specialized ferry breakdown response team.

headline: WA Democrats drop push for business tax to cover Medicaid costs
summary: Washington Democrats abandoned a proposed employer Medicaid tax projected to raise over $950 million starting in the 2027-29 biennium to address federal Medicaid cuts that will cost the state billions, citing procedural complexity during the short session; lawmakers are instead pursuing smaller revenue alternatives—a $10 million insurer assessment for abortion services and a $56 million B&O tax break rollback for insurers—that are included in current budget plans.

headline: Iowa Senate sends K-12 funding package to governor’s desk
summary: Iowa lawmakers approved a 2% State Supplemental Aid increase for K-12 schools for 2026-2027, representing a compromise between House and Senate Republicans after initial proposals of 2.25% and 1.75% respectively. The legislation includes $7 million for paraeducator pay, a $1 million cap on transportation equity payments, and a new biannual student enrollment calculation system. Democrats criticized the 2% rate as inadequate, citing program cuts in districts like Boone and Ballard, while linking the shortfall to expanded funding for the Education Savings Account private school voucher program. Republicans defended the measure as providing $111.5 million in new education dollars, raising per-pupil funding to $8,148. The bill passed the Senate 27-20 and awaits Governor Reynolds' approval.

headline: The Rev. Jesse Jackson to lie in state at SC Statehouse
summary: The South Carolina Statehouse will honor civil rights leader Rev. Jesse Jackson Sr. by having his body lie in state on March 2, with Gov. Henry McMaster ordering flags at half-staff. Jackson's early activism included a July 1960 peaceful protest at a whites-only Greenville public library as part of the "Greenville Eight," which successfully forced integration of the library two months later.

headline: Local Lookout: Hospital loan denied | $3M for wildfires | New BLM boss?
summary: The Natrona Collective Health Trust denied a $29.2 million loan request from Memorial Hospital of Converse County to purchase Summit Medical Center in Casper, citing a binding agreement with Banner Wyoming Medical Center that prohibits financial support to competing healthcare providers in the same geographic market, raising policy concerns about regional healthcare competition and access.

headline: Ahead of State of the Union, Marist Poll shows 60% say country worse off than a year ago
summary: A February Marist Institute poll of 1,462 Americans found 60% believe the country is worse off under President Trump, with 55% saying he is changing the country for the worse—a significant increase from 42% in 2018. The survey shows sharp partisan divides: 89% of Democrats and 64% of independents view his performance negatively versus 13% of Republicans, and 58% of independents report personal negative impacts from his policies. Key findings include 78% seeing a "serious threat to democracy" and 68% saying checks and balances are not working well. The results could impact midterm elections, particularly in North Carolina's 11th district where Democrats are targeting Republican Rep. Chuck Edwards. Pessimism has risen steadily since March 2025, when 48% said Trump was changing the country for the worse.
